1:
"The Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him, to shew unto his servants things which must shortly come to pass; and he sent and signified it by his angel unto his servant John:" The witnesses note the opening of the Revelation given by God through Christ. On "the Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him," the grammatical voices note the chain of disclosure. Barnes and Robertson note the source: "the Revelation (apokalypsis — an unveiling, disclosure) of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him" — the book is an unveiling of things hidden; it belongs to Christ, given to him by the Father, to be made known to his servants; the divine chain — God to Christ to the angel to John.

On "to shew unto his servants things which must shortly come to pass," interpreters note the timing. Barnes and Ellicott note that the things "must shortly come to pass (en tachei — quickly, soon)"; the phrase is read by some as imminence (the events soon to begin) and by others as swiftness once begun; the bearing on the book's interpretation is noted.

On "he sent and signified it by his angel unto his servant John," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede note the mediation: Christ "sent and signified (esemanen — showed by signs, symbols) it by his angel unto his servant John" — the revelation came through an angel and was given in signs and symbols; the symbolic character of the book is marked from the outset. The verse displays the opening of the Revelation given by God through Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the chain — "the Revelation (unveiling) of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him," the disclosure belonging to Christ, given by the Father, made known to his servants (Barnes, Robertson); "things which must shortly come to pass (quickly, soon)," the timing read as imminence or swiftness once begun (Barnes, Ellicott); "signified it by his angel," the revelation in signs and symbols through an angel (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the unveiling. The verse marks the source: God through Christ. The verse displays the purpose: shew his servants. The verse displays the timing: shortly. The verse displays the means: signified by an angel. The opening displays the symbolic revelation given by God through Christ. The verse displays the Revelation: of Christ. The verse displays the servants: shown. The verse displays the angel: mediating. The opening of the Revelation given by God through Christ displays the title and chain of the book: the unveiling of Jesus Christ, given him by God, to show his servants things shortly to come, signified by his angel to John. The verse displays the signified revelation, to which John bore record (v.2).
2:
"Who bare record of the word of God, and of the testimony of Jesus Christ, and of all things that he saw." The brief verse notes John bearing record of the word and testimony of Christ. The grammatical voices note the witness. Barnes and Robertson note John's role as witness: "who bare record (witnessed, testified) of the word of God, and of the testimony of Jesus Christ, and of all things that he saw" — John faithfully testified to all he received: the word of God, the testimony of Jesus, and the visions shown him.

On "the word of God, and the testimony of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the content. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the word of God" and "the testimony of Jesus Christ" are the substance of John's witness — the divine message and the witness Christ bore (or the witness borne to Christ); the same phrases recur as the ground of John's exile (v.9).

On "all things that he saw," the grammatical-historical note the visions: John bore record "of all things that he saw" — the entire content of the visions; he wrote down faithfully what was shown him; the apostle is the faithful recorder of the revelation. The verse displays John bearing record of the word and testimony of Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the witness — "who bare record (testified) of the word of God, and of the testimony of Jesus Christ, and of all things that he saw," John faithfully testifying to the word, the testimony, and the visions (Barnes, Robertson); "the word of God, and the testimony of Jesus Christ," the substance of his witness, recurring at v.9 (Barnes, Ellicott); "all things that he saw," John the faithful recorder of the visions (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the faithful witness. The verse marks the record: borne by John. The verse displays the word: of God. The verse displays the testimony: of Jesus. The verse displays the visions: all he saw. The witness displays John recording the revelation faithfully. The verse displays the record: borne. The verse displays the testimony: of Christ. The verse displays the seeing: faithful. John bearing record of the word and testimony of Christ displays the apostle's faithful witness: he testified to the word of God, the testimony of Jesus Christ, and all the things he saw in the visions. The verse displays the faithful record, opening the beatitude on readers and hearers (v.3).
3:
"Blessed is he that readeth, and they that hear the words of this prophecy, and keep those things which are written therein: for the time is at hand." The witnesses note the beatitude on those who read, hear, and keep the prophecy. On "blessed is he that readeth, and they that hear the words of this prophecy," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Barnes and Robertson note the first beatitude of the book: "blessed is he that readeth (the one who reads aloud, perhaps in the assembly), and they that hear (the congregation) the words of this prophecy" — a blessing is pronounced on the public reader and the hearers of the book; the reading and hearing of the prophecy bring blessing.

On "and keep those things which are written therein," the witnesses note the obedience. Barnes and Henry note that the blessing belongs especially to those who "keep (observe, obey, treasure) those things which are written therein" — not mere hearing but keeping; the book is not for curiosity but for obedience; the blessing rests on doing, not only hearing.

On "for the time is at hand," the grammatical-historical note the nearness: "for the time is at hand (near)" — the reason for the blessing and the urgency is that the time of the things foretold is near; the imminence (read as the events soon to begin, or near in God's reckoning) presses the call to heed the prophecy. The verse displays the beatitude on those who read, hear, and keep the prophecy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "blessed is he that readeth, and they that hear the words of this prophecy," a blessing on the reader and hearers (Barnes, Robertson); "and keep those things which are written therein," the blessing especially on those who obey, not merely hear (Barnes, Henry); "for the time is at hand," the nearness of the foretold time pressing the call to heed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beatitude. The verse marks the reader: blessed. The verse displays the hearers: blessed. The verse displays the keeping: obeyed. The verse displays the reason: the time is near. The blessing displays the reader, hearer, and keeper of the prophecy. The verse displays the reading: blessed. The verse displays the keeping: required. The verse displays the time: at hand. The beatitude on those who read, hear, and keep the prophecy displays the book's opening blessing: blessed is the one who reads and those who hear and keep the words of this prophecy — for the time is at hand. The verse displays the blessed hearers, addressed in the greeting to the seven churches (v.4).
4:
"John to the seven churches which are in Asia: Grace be unto you, and peace, from him which is, and which was, and which is to come; and from the seven Spirits which are before his throne;" The witnesses note the greeting from the eternal God and the seven Spirits — the crux of the seven Spirits. On "John to the seven churches which are in Asia," the grammatical voices note the recipients. Barnes and Robertson note the address: "John to the seven churches which are in Asia (proconsular Asia, the seven named in v.11)" — John writes to seven actual churches in Asia Minor; the number seven, the number of completeness, suggests they also represent the whole church.

On "from him which is, and which was, and which is to come," the witnesses note the eternal Father. Barnes and Ellicott note the title of God: "from him which is, and which was, and which is to come (the eternal, unchanging God, the everlasting I AM, in past, present, and future)" — the first source of the blessing is the eternal God, the Father.

On "the seven Spirits which are before his throne," interpreters divide. The Reformed Gill and the traditional Trinitarian reading hold that "the seven Spirits" is the Holy Spirit, represented under a sevenfold figure (the fullness and perfection of the one Spirit, sevenfold in his operations); the placement in the Trinitarian salutation (Father, the seven Spirits, Christ, v.5) strongly favours this. The grammatical-historical Barnes inclines to seven angels (or the principal "angels of the presence") that stand before the throne, citing the difficulty of calling the Holy Spirit "seven Spirits" and the parallel with the seven angels of 8:2. The verse displays the greeting from the eternal God and the seven Spirits.

The witnesses agree the substance: John greets the seven churches with grace and peace from the eternal God who is, was, and is to come, and from the seven Spirits before the throne (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott). But "the seven Spirits" divides them: the Holy Spirit in sevenfold fullness (Gill, the Trinitarian reading) or seven angels before the throne (Barnes). The verse displays the seven-Spirits crux. The verse marks the recipients: seven churches. The verse displays the Father: who is, was, and is to come. The verse displays the debate: the Holy Spirit or seven angels. The verse displays the throne: before which they stand. The greeting displays grace from the eternal God and the seven Spirits. The verse displays the churches: seven. The verse displays the God: eternal. The verse displays the Spirits: seven. The greeting from the eternal God and the seven Spirits displays the salutation and a crux: grace and peace from the eternal God who is, was, and is to come, and from the seven Spirits before his throne — read as the Holy Spirit in sevenfold fullness (Gill, the Trinitarian salutation) or as seven angels of the presence (Barnes). The verse displays the eternal God, joined by Jesus Christ the faithful witness (v.5).
5:
"And from Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, and the first begotten of the dead, and the prince of the kings of the earth. Unto him that loved us, and washed us from our sins in his own blood," The witnesses note the threefold title of Christ and the doxology to him who washed us. On "from Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness," the grammatical voices note the first title. Barnes and Robertson note the threefold description: "Jesus Christ, who is the faithful (true, trustworthy) witness (martys)" — Christ is the faithful witness, who bore true testimony to God's truth, even unto death; the model of faithful testimony for the persecuted churches.

On "the first begotten of the dead, and the prince of the kings of the earth," the witnesses note the second and third titles. Barnes and Ellicott note: "the first begotten (firstborn, prototokos) of the dead (the first to rise to immortal life, the pledge of the resurrection), and the prince (ruler, archon) of the kings of the earth (the sovereign over all earthly rulers)" — Christ is risen and reigns supreme; the three titles span his faithful witness, his resurrection, and his universal dominion.

On "unto him that loved us, and washed us from our sins in his own blood," the eastern Cyril notes the doxology: the mention of Christ breaks into praise — "unto him that loved us, and washed (or loosed, lysanti) us from our sins in (by) his own blood" — Christ's love and his cleansing of believers from sin by his blood call forth adoration; the redeeming love and atoning blood are the ground of the doxology. The verse displays the threefold title of Christ and the doxology to him who washed us.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the titles — "Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, and the first begotten of the dead, and the prince of the kings of the earth," the three titles of Christ's witness, resurrection, and dominion (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott); "unto him that loved us, and washed (loosed) us from our sins in his own blood," the doxology grounded in Christ's love and atoning blood (Cyril). The verse displays the exalted Christ. The verse marks the witness: faithful. The verse displays the resurrection: firstborn of the dead. The verse displays the dominion: prince of kings. The verse displays the love: washed us in his blood. The titles display Christ the faithful, risen, reigning Redeemer. The verse displays the witness: true. The verse displays the firstborn: of the dead. The verse displays the blood: cleansing. The threefold title of Christ and the doxology to him who washed us display the exalted Redeemer: Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, the prince of the kings of the earth — who loved us and washed us from our sins in his own blood. The verse displays the redeeming Christ, who made us kings and priests (v.6).
6:
"And hath made us kings and priests unto God and his Father; to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen." The brief verse notes that Christ made us kings and priests, with the doxology of glory and dominion. The grammatical voices note the believers' dignity. Barnes and Robertson note the result of redemption: "hath made us kings and priests (a kingdom of priests, a royal priesthood) unto God and his Father" — Christ has made the redeemed kings and priests to God; the dignity of believers, raised to royal and priestly standing (cf. 1 Peter 2:9; Exodus 19:6).

On "kings and priests," the witnesses note the twofold honour. Barnes and Ellicott note the meaning (some texts: "a kingdom, priests"): believers share Christ's royalty (they reign with him) and his priesthood (they offer spiritual sacrifices and have access to God); the redeemed are exalted to this double dignity.

On "to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen," the eastern Cyril notes the ascription: the doxology ascribes to Christ "glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen" — eternal glory and power are rendered to the Redeemer; the praise that the mention of his love and redemption evokes is sealed with "Amen." The verse displays that Christ made us kings and priests, with the doxology of glory and dominion.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dignity — "hath made us kings and priests (a kingdom of priests) unto God and his Father," the redeemed raised to royal and priestly standing (1 Peter 2:9, Barnes, Robertson); believers sharing Christ's royalty and priesthood (Barnes, Ellicott); "to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever. Amen," eternal glory and power ascribed to Christ (Cyril). The verse displays the doxology. The verse marks the dignity: kings and priests. The verse displays the standing: unto God. The verse displays the ascription: glory and dominion. The verse displays the seal: Amen. The doxology displays the redeemed exalted and Christ glorified. The verse displays the kings: made. The verse displays the priests: unto God. The verse displays the glory: to Christ. That Christ made us kings and priests, with the doxology of glory and dominion, displays the dignity of the redeemed and the praise of the Redeemer: he has made us kings and priests to God his Father — to him be glory and dominion forever. The verse displays the glorified Christ, who comes with clouds (v.7).
7:
"Behold, he cometh with clouds; and every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him: and all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him. Even so, Amen." The witnesses note the announcement of Christ's coming with clouds, seen by all. On "behold, he cometh with clouds," the grammatical voices note the advent. Barnes and Robertson note the proclamation of the second coming: "behold, he cometh with clouds" — the great theme of the book is announced: Christ comes with clouds (the symbol of divine majesty and judgment, cf. Daniel 7:13; Matthew 24:30); his glorious return.

On "every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him," the witnesses note the universal sight. Barnes and Ellicott note that "every eye shall see him (the coming is visible, universal), and they also which pierced him (those who crucified him, or those responsible, the Jews and all sinners)" — all will behold him, including those who pierced him; the visible, universal manifestation of the once-crucified Lord.

On "all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him," the grammatical-historical note the mourning: "all kindreds (tribes) of the earth shall wail (mourn, lament) because of him" — at his coming the tribes of the earth will mourn, whether in penitence or in terror at the judgment; the universal lament at the appearing of the Judge. "Even so, Amen" seals the certainty. The verse displays the announcement of Christ's coming with clouds, seen by all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the advent — "behold, he cometh with clouds," the second coming with clouds of majesty and judgment (Daniel 7:13, Barnes, Robertson); "every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him," the visible universal coming, beheld even by those who pierced him (Barnes, Ellicott); "all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him," the tribes mourning at the Judge's appearing, sealed "Even so, Amen" (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the coming Christ. The verse marks the coming: with clouds. The verse displays the sight: every eye. The verse displays the pierced: they too see him. The verse displays the wailing: all kindreds. The announcement displays the universal, visible coming of the Judge. The verse displays the clouds: of coming. The verse displays the eye: seeing. The verse displays the wailing: of the earth. The announcement of Christ's coming with clouds, seen by all, displays the keynote of the book: behold, he comes with clouds, and every eye shall see him, even those who pierced him, and all the tribes of the earth shall wail because of him. The verse displays the coming Lord, who is the Alpha and Omega (v.8).
8:
"I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come, the Almighty." The witnesses note the divine self-declaration: Alpha and Omega, the Almighty. On "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending," the grammatical voices note the title. Barnes and Robertson note the self-designation: "I am Alpha and Omega (the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet), the beginning and the ending" — the speaker declares himself the first and the last, the beginning and end of all; the title of eternity and all-encompassing being; interpreters note it is spoken by "the Lord God" (and applied also to Christ, v.11; 22:13).

On "saith the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come," the witnesses note the eternity. Barnes and Ellicott note the title repeated from v.4: "the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come" — the eternal, unchanging God, existing in past, present, and future; the everlasting one.

On "the Almighty," the grammatical-historical note the omnipotence: "the Almighty (pantokrator — the all-powerful, the ruler of all)" — the speaker is the omnipotent God; his almighty power undergirds the certainty of all that the book foretells; the title combines eternity (Alpha and Omega, is/was/is to come) with omnipotence (the Almighty). The verse displays the divine self-declaration: Alpha and Omega, the Almighty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the title — "I am Alpha and Omega (the first and last), the beginning and the ending," the title of eternity and all-encompassing being, spoken by the Lord God and applied to Christ (22:13, Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come," the eternal unchanging God (Barnes, Ellicott); "the Almighty," the omnipotent ruler of all (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eternal Almighty. The verse marks the title: Alpha and Omega. The verse displays the scope: beginning and ending. The verse displays the eternity: is, was, is to come. The verse displays the power: the Almighty. The declaration displays the eternal, almighty God. The verse displays the Alpha: and Omega. The verse displays the Lord: eternal. The verse displays the Almighty: omnipotent. The divine self-declaration — Alpha and Omega, the Almighty — displays the majesty undergirding the book: "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending," says the Lord, the eternal one who is, was, and is to come, the Almighty. The verse displays the Almighty God, witnessed by John on Patmos (v.9).
9:
"I John, who also am your brother, and companion in tribulation, and in the kingdom and patience of Jesus Christ, was in the isle that is called Patmos, for the word of God, and for the testimony of Jesus Christ." The witnesses note John on Patmos, a brother and companion in tribulation. On "I John, who also am your brother, and companion in tribulation," the grammatical voices note the humble fellowship. Barnes and Robertson note John's self-identification: "I John, who also am your brother (not lording it over them), and companion (sharer, fellow-partaker) in tribulation (affliction, persecution)" — John names himself simply as a brother and fellow-sufferer; he shares the churches' trials, not above them.

On "in the kingdom and patience of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the threefold fellowship. Barnes and Henry note that John is companion "in the kingdom (the reign of Christ, present and coming) and patience (patient endurance) of Jesus Christ" — tribulation, kingdom, and patient endurance go together; the believer shares Christ's sufferings, his kingdom, and the patience that endures until the kingdom comes.

On "was in the isle that is called Patmos, for the word of God, and for the testimony of Jesus Christ," the grammatical-historical note the exile: John "was in the isle... Patmos (a rocky island in the Aegean, a place of banishment), for the word of God, and for the testimony of Jesus Christ" — John was exiled to Patmos on account of his faithful preaching and witness; his suffering for the gospel was the occasion of the vision. The verse displays John on Patmos, a brother and companion in tribulation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fellowship — "I John, who also am your brother, and companion (fellow-sharer) in tribulation," John a brother and fellow-sufferer, not above the churches (Barnes, Robertson); "in the kingdom and patience of Jesus Christ," tribulation, kingdom, and patient endurance together (Barnes, Henry); "in the isle that is called Patmos, for the word of God," John exiled for his faithful witness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exiled apostle. The verse marks the brother: John. The verse displays the fellowship: in tribulation. The verse displays the kingdom: and patience. The verse displays the exile: Patmos. The fellowship displays John sharing the churches' trials on Patmos. The verse displays the companion: in affliction. The verse displays the Patmos: exile. The verse displays the testimony: of Jesus. John on Patmos, a brother and companion in tribulation, displays the humble setting of the vision: John, a brother and fellow-partaker in the tribulation, kingdom, and patience of Jesus Christ, was on the isle of Patmos for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus. The verse displays the exiled witness, caught up in the Spirit on the Lord's day (v.10).
10:
"I was in the Spirit on the Lord's day, and heard behind me a great voice, as of a trumpet," The witnesses note John in the Spirit on the Lord's day — the crux of its meaning. On "I was in the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the prophetic ecstasy. Barnes and Robertson note the state: "I was (came to be) in the Spirit (in an ecstatic, prophetic condition, under the Spirit's special influence)" — John was caught up by the Holy Spirit into a state of prophetic vision, abstracted from ordinary consciousness (cf. Acts 10:10; 2 Corinthians 12:2).

On "on the Lord's day," interpreters divide over the meaning. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with Gill and Clarke, hold "the Lord's day (kyriake hemera)" is the first day of the week, Sunday — the Christian Lord's Day, so called in honour of Christ's resurrection (the same adjective kyriakos used of "the Lord's Supper," 1 Corinthians 11:20; the day called the Lord's day by Ignatius and the early fathers); Ellicott: "there is no ground whatever for the futurist interpretation that this expression refers to the Day of the Lord"; Robertson: it "has no reference to hemera kyriou (the day of judgment)." The futurist reading (named and rejected by Ellicott and Robertson) takes it as the eschatological "day of the Lord," into which John was transported in vision.

On "heard behind me a great voice, as of a trumpet," the grammatical-historical note the summons: John "heard behind me a great (loud) voice, as of a trumpet (clear, loud, and commanding, like Christ's, v.12-13)" — a sudden, loud, trumpet-clear voice from behind, the voice of Christ summoning his attention. The verse displays John in the Spirit on the Lord's day.

The witnesses agree the substance: John, on the Lord's day, was caught into a prophetic ecstasy by the Spirit and heard a loud, trumpet-like voice (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott). But "the Lord's day" divides them: the consensus (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Gill, Clarke) takes it as Sunday, the Christian Lord's Day; the futurist reading (named and rejected) takes it as the eschatological day of the Lord. The verse displays the Lord's-day crux. The verse marks the state: in the Spirit. The verse displays the debate: Sunday or the day of the Lord. The verse displays the voice: as a trumpet. The verse displays the summons: from behind. The vision displays John rapt in the Spirit on the Lord's day. The verse displays the Spirit: rapt. The verse displays the day: the Lord's. The verse displays the voice: trumpet-loud. John in the Spirit on the Lord's day displays the setting of the vision and a crux: rapt in the Spirit on the Lord's day — read by the consensus as Sunday, the Christian Lord's Day (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Gill), and rejected when taken (by the futurists) as the eschatological day of the Lord — John heard a great trumpet-like voice behind him. The verse displays the trumpet voice, which bids him write to the seven churches (v.11).
11:
"Saying, I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last: and, What thou seest, write in a book, and send it unto the seven churches which are in Asia; unto Ephesus, and unto Smyrna, and unto Pergamos, and unto Thyatira, and unto Sardis, and unto Philadelphia, and unto Laodicea." The witnesses note the command to write to the seven churches by name. On "I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last," the grammatical voices note the speaker. Barnes and Robertson note that the voice declares itself: "I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last" — the speaker (Christ, as v.17-18 shows) identifies himself with the divine title of v.8; the first and last, applied to Christ, affirms his deity. (Many texts omit the opening clause, but the title recurs in v.17.)

On "what thou seest, write in a book," the witnesses note the charge. Barnes and Ellicott note the command: "what thou seest, write in a book (scroll), and send it unto the seven churches which are in Asia" — John is to record the visions and send the written account to the seven churches; the book is a circular letter to all seven.

On the seven named churches, the grammatical-historical note the list: "Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamos, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea" — seven actual churches in proconsular Asia, named in the geographical order of a circuit; to each a particular message follows (chs. 2-3). The verse displays the command to write to the seven churches by name.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the speaker — "I am Alpha and Omega, the first and the last," Christ identifying himself with the divine title, affirming his deity (Barnes, Robertson); "what thou seest, write in a book, and send it unto the seven churches," John to record and send the visions to the seven (Barnes, Ellicott); the seven named churches of Asia in circuit order, each to receive a message (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the command. The verse marks the speaker: Alpha and Omega. The verse displays the charge: write in a book. The verse displays the recipients: seven churches. The verse displays the names: Ephesus to Laodicea. The command displays John writing to the seven named churches. The verse displays the title: first and last. The verse displays the writing: commanded. The verse displays the churches: seven. The command to write to the seven churches by name displays the commission: the voice, Alpha and Omega, bids John write what he sees and send it to the seven churches of Asia — Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamos, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea. The verse displays the seven churches, symbolized by golden candlesticks (v.12).
12:
"And I turned to see the voice that spake with me. And being turned, I saw seven golden candlesticks;" The brief verse notes the vision of the seven golden candlesticks. The grammatical voices note the turning. Barnes and Robertson note John's response to the voice: "I turned to see the voice (the one whose voice it was) that spake with me. And being turned, I saw seven golden candlesticks (lampstands)" — John turns toward the voice and sees seven golden lampstands; the first object of the vision.

On "seven golden candlesticks," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "candlesticks (lampstands, lychniai)" are golden — precious, costly; they are explained (v.20) as the seven churches; the church is a lampstand, holding up the light of the gospel in the world.

On the symbolism, the puritan Henry notes the meaning: the golden lampstands represent the churches as bearers of light — their office is to give light, to hold forth the word; gold marks their preciousness to Christ; and the seven, the completeness of the church. The verse displays the vision of the seven golden candlesticks.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turning — "I turned to see the voice that spake with me. And being turned, I saw seven golden candlesticks (lampstands)," John turning toward the voice and seeing seven golden lampstands (Barnes, Robertson); the golden lampstands explained as the seven churches (v.20), the church a lampstand bearing the gospel light (Barnes, Ellicott); the gold marking their preciousness, the seven their completeness (Henry). The verse displays the lampstands. The verse marks the turning: to see the voice. The verse displays the vision: seven candlesticks. The verse displays the material: golden. The verse displays the meaning: the churches. The vision displays the churches as golden lampstands bearing light. The verse displays the candlesticks: golden. The verse displays the seven: churches. The verse displays the light: held forth. The vision of the seven golden candlesticks displays the first object of John's vision: turning toward the voice, he saw seven golden lampstands — the seven churches, precious to Christ and set to bear the light of the gospel. The verse displays the golden lampstands, amid which stands the glorified Christ (v.13).
13:
"And in the midst of the seven candlesticks one like unto the Son of man, clothed with a garment down to the foot, and girt about the paps with a golden girdle." The witnesses note the glorified Son of man among the candlesticks. On "in the midst of the seven candlesticks one like unto the Son of man," the grammatical voices note the central figure. Barnes and Robertson note the appearance of Christ: "in the midst of the seven candlesticks (lampstands) one like unto the Son of man (homoion huion anthropou, the title from Daniel 7:13)" — Christ stands in the midst of the churches; the title "Son of man" marks him as the glorified, exalted one of Daniel's vision; he walks among his churches.

On "in the midst," the witnesses note the presence. Barnes and Cyril note that Christ "in the midst" of the lampstands signifies his presence with and among his churches; he is not absent but stands in their midst, watching, sustaining, and judging them.

On "clothed with a garment down to the foot, and girt about the paps with a golden girdle," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the priestly-kingly dress: Christ is "clothed with a garment down to the foot (a long robe, the dress of the high priest and of dignity), and girt about the paps (the breast) with a golden girdle (a symbol of royal and priestly office)" — the long robe and golden girdle mark Christ's priestly and kingly dignity; he appears in the midst of his churches as their royal High Priest. The verse displays the glorified Son of man among the candlesticks.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the central figure — "in the midst of the seven candlesticks one like unto the Son of man," Christ standing among the churches, the glorified Son of man of Daniel 7:13 (Barnes, Robertson); "in the midst," Christ's presence with his churches, watching and sustaining (Barnes, Cyril); "clothed with a garment down to the foot... a golden girdle," the long robe and golden girdle of priestly and kingly dignity (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the priestly-kingly Christ. The verse marks the figure: Son of man. The verse displays the place: midst of the candlesticks. The verse displays the robe: to the foot. The verse displays the girdle: golden. The vision displays Christ the royal High Priest among his churches. The verse displays the Son of man: glorified. The verse displays the garment: priestly. The verse displays the girdle: kingly. The glorified Son of man among the candlesticks displays the central vision: in the midst of the seven lampstands stood one like the Son of man, clothed in a long robe and girded with a golden girdle — Christ present among his churches as their royal High Priest. The verse displays the majestic Christ, whose head and hair are white as snow (v.14).
14:
"His head and his hairs were white like wool, as white as snow; and his eyes were as a flame of fire;" The brief verse notes the white head and flaming eyes of the glorified Christ. The grammatical voices note the symbolism of the description. Barnes and Robertson note the first features: "his head and his hairs were white like wool, as white as snow" — the whiteness of his head and hair (drawn from Daniel 7:9, the Ancient of Days) signifies eternity, purity, and the venerable majesty of age; the glorified Christ shares the appearance of the Ancient of Days.

On "white like wool, as white as snow," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note that the dazzling whiteness marks his holiness and eternal, unchanging being; the application to Christ of the imagery of the Ancient of Days affirms his deity and eternity.

On "his eyes were as a flame of fire," the grammatical-historical note the piercing gaze: "his eyes were as a flame of fire" — his eyes blaze like fire, symbolizing his penetrating, all-searching knowledge and his consuming judgment; nothing is hidden from his sight; the flaming eyes search the churches (cf. 2:18, 23). The verse displays the white head and flaming eyes of the glorified Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the features — "his head and his hairs were white like wool, as white as snow," the whiteness (Daniel 7:9) signifying eternity, purity, and venerable majesty (Barnes, Robertson); the dazzling whiteness marking holiness and eternal being, the Ancient-of-Days imagery affirming his deity (Barnes, Ellicott); "his eyes were as a flame of fire," the blazing eyes of penetrating knowledge and consuming judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the majestic features. The verse marks the head: white as snow. The verse displays the meaning: eternity, purity. The verse displays the eyes: flame of fire. The verse displays the gaze: all-searching. The description displays the eternal, all-seeing Christ. The verse displays the hair: white. The verse displays the eyes: flaming. The verse displays the searching: complete. The white head and flaming eyes of the glorified Christ display the symbolic majesty of the vision: his head and hair white as snow, marking his eternity and purity (the Ancient of Days), and his eyes as a flame of fire, searching all things in penetrating judgment. The verse displays the searching Christ, with feet of burnished brass (v.15).
15:
"And his feet like unto fine brass, as if they burned in a furnace; and his voice as the sound of many waters." The brief verse notes the feet of burnished brass and voice of many waters. The grammatical voices note the imagery. Barnes and Robertson note the further features: "his feet like unto fine brass (chalkolibanon — burnished or glowing brass/bronze), as if they burned in a furnace" — his feet shine like brass glowing in a furnace; the symbol of strength, stability, and the splendour of his glorified body; some see in the glowing feet the symbol of his treading down enemies in judgment.

On "fine brass, as if they burned in a furnace," the witnesses note the strength and judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that the glowing brass feet signify firmness and the consuming purity of judgment; Christ's feet are strong to crush and pure as refined metal; he walks among the churches with feet of judgment.

On "his voice as the sound of many waters," the grammatical-historical note the majestic voice: "his voice as the sound of many waters (the roar of a great cataract or the sea)" — his voice is mighty, full, and majestic, like the roar of many waters; the overwhelming power and majesty of his speech (cf. Ezekiel 43:2). The verse displays the feet of burnished brass and voice of many waters.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the imagery — "his feet like unto fine brass (glowing bronze), as if they burned in a furnace," the feet shining like brass in a furnace, the symbol of strength and the splendour of judgment (Barnes, Robertson); the glowing brass signifying firmness and consuming purity, feet of judgment (Barnes, Ellicott); "his voice as the sound of many waters," the mighty, majestic voice like a great cataract (Ezekiel 43:2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mighty features. The verse marks the feet: burnished brass. The verse displays the meaning: strength, judgment. The verse displays the voice: many waters. The verse displays the majesty: overwhelming. The description displays the strong, majestic, judging Christ. The verse displays the feet: glowing. The verse displays the brass: refined. The verse displays the voice: mighty. The feet of burnished brass and voice of many waters display the strength and majesty of the glorified Christ: his feet glowing like brass refined in a furnace, strong for judgment, and his voice like the roar of many waters, mighty and overwhelming. The verse displays the mighty Christ, holding the seven stars (v.16).
16:
"And he had in his right hand seven stars: and out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword: and his countenance was as the sun shineth in his strength." The witnesses note the seven stars, the sword of his mouth, and the shining countenance. On "in his right hand seven stars," the grammatical voices note the held stars. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "he had in his right hand seven stars" — Christ holds seven stars in his right hand; explained (v.20) as the angels (ministers) of the seven churches; he holds and upholds them, the symbol of his protecting power and ownership.

On "out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword," the witnesses note the word of judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that "out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword (rhomphaia, a long sword)" — the sword proceeding from his mouth is the word of Christ, sharp and piercing, dividing and judging (cf. Hebrews 4:12; Isaiah 49:2); his word both saves and smites; the sword of judgment against his enemies.

On "his countenance was as the sun shineth in his strength," the grammatical-historical note the dazzling glory: "his countenance (face) was as the sun shineth in his strength (in full, noonday brightness)" — his face blazes like the sun at full strength; the unbearable, dazzling glory of the glorified Christ (cf. the Transfiguration, Matthew 17:2); the splendour that overwhelms the beholder. The verse displays the seven stars, the sword of his mouth, and the shining countenance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the stars — "in his right hand seven stars," Christ holding the angels/ministers of the churches, the symbol of his protecting ownership (v.20, Barnes, Robertson); "out of his mouth went a sharp twoedged sword," the word of Christ, sharp to judge and divide (Hebrews 4:12, Barnes, Ellicott); "his countenance was as the sun shineth in his strength," the dazzling noonday glory of the glorified Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the glorious Christ. The verse marks the hand: seven stars. The verse displays the mouth: a sharp sword. The verse displays the countenance: as the sun. The verse displays the glory: dazzling. The description displays Christ holding the churches, his word a sword, his face the sun. The verse displays the stars: held. The verse displays the sword: of his word. The verse displays the face: shining. The seven stars, the sword of his mouth, and the shining countenance display the climax of the vision: Christ holds the seven stars in his right hand, a sharp two-edged sword goes from his mouth, and his face shines like the sun at full strength — the glorified Lord, holder of the churches, judge by his word, dazzling in glory. The verse displays the overwhelming glory, before which John falls as dead (v.17).
17:
"And when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead. And he laid his right hand upon me, saying unto me, Fear not; I am the first and the last:" The witnesses note John falling as dead and Christ's reassuring "Fear not." On "when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead," the grammatical voices note the overwhelming effect. Barnes and Robertson note the prostration: "when I saw him, I fell at his feet as (one) dead" — the overwhelming glory of the vision struck John down; the natural effect of beholding the divine majesty is prostration and faintness (cf. Ezekiel 1:28; Daniel 8:17; 10:9); the apostle is overcome.

On "he laid his right hand upon me, saying... Fear not," the witnesses note the gracious touch. Barnes and Henry note the comfort: "he laid his right hand upon me (the hand that held the stars), saying... Fear not" — the same Christ whose glory overwhelmed him touches and reassures him; the tender "Fear not" calms the terror; the majestic Lord is also the gracious comforter.

On "I am the first and the last," the grammatical-historical note the ground of comfort: the reassurance rests on the title "I am the first and the last" — the divine title (from Isaiah 44:6; 48:12), applied to Christ, affirming his deity and eternity; the eternal one need not be feared by his own; his everlasting being is the ground of the believer's confidence. The verse displays John falling as dead and Christ's reassuring "Fear not."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prostration — "when I saw him, I fell at his feet as dead," the overwhelming glory striking John down, the natural effect of beholding divine majesty (Daniel 10:9, Barnes, Robertson); "he laid his right hand upon me, saying... Fear not," Christ's gracious touch and reassurance calming the terror (Barnes, Henry); "I am the first and the last," the divine title affirming Christ's deity and eternity, the ground of comfort (Isaiah 44:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reassured apostle. The verse marks the falling: as dead. The verse displays the touch: his right hand. The verse displays the comfort: Fear not. The verse displays the title: first and last. The vision displays the overwhelmed John reassured by the eternal Christ. The verse displays the prostration: complete. The verse displays the hand: laid on him. The verse displays the title: divine. John falling as dead and Christ's reassuring "Fear not" display the tender heart of the majestic vision: overcome by the glory, John fell as dead, but Christ laid his right hand on him and said, "Fear not; I am the first and the last" — the eternal Lord both awe-inspiring and gracious. The verse displays the reassuring Christ, who holds the keys of death (v.18).
18:
"I am he that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore, Amen; and have the keys of hell and of death." The witnesses note Christ the living one who holds the keys of death and hell. On "I am he that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore," the grammatical voices note the resurrection. Barnes and Robertson note the self-declaration: "I am he that liveth (the living one), and was dead (became dead, died); and, behold, I am alive for evermore (alive unto the ages of the ages)" — Christ is the living one who died and now lives forever; his death and triumphant resurrection are the ground of his reassurance and authority.

On "I am alive for evermore, Amen," the witnesses note the endless life. Barnes and Ellicott note that Christ, having died, now lives eternally; death is behind him forever; his endless life secures the life of his people; "Amen" seals the certainty.

On "have the keys of hell and of death," the grammatical-historical note the authority: Christ has "the keys of hell (hades, the unseen world of the dead) and of death" — he holds the keys, the symbol of authority and control over death and the realm of the dead; the risen Lord has power over death and hades, opening and shutting as he wills; the comfort that the believer's death and destiny are in his hands. The verse displays Christ the living one who holds the keys of death and hell.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resurrection — "I am he that liveth, and was dead; and, behold, I am alive for evermore," Christ the living one who died and lives forever (Barnes, Robertson); his endless life securing his people's life, "Amen" sealing it (Barnes, Ellicott); "have the keys of hell and of death," Christ's authority over death and hades, opening and shutting at will (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the living Lord. The verse marks the life: he that liveth. The verse displays the death: was dead. The verse displays the resurrection: alive forevermore. The verse displays the authority: keys of death and hell. The declaration displays Christ risen and sovereign over death. The verse displays the living: one. The verse displays the death: conquered. The verse displays the keys: held. Christ the living one who holds the keys of death and hell displays the ground of the believer's hope: "I am he that liveth, and was dead; behold, I am alive for evermore, and have the keys of hell and of death" — the risen Christ, master of death and the grave. The verse displays the keys of death, framing the threefold division of the book (v.19).
19:
"Write the things which thou hast seen, and the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter;" The brief verse notes the threefold command structuring the book. The grammatical voices note the division. Barnes and Robertson note the comprehensive charge: "write the things which thou hast seen (the vision just described, ch. 1), and the things which are (the present state of the churches, chs. 2-3), and the things which shall be hereafter (meta tauta — after these, the future things, chs. 4-22)" — the command gives a threefold outline of the book: the vision seen, the present, and the future.

On "the things which thou hast seen," the witnesses note the past vision. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the things which thou hast seen" refers to the vision of the glorified Christ just received; John is to record what he has already beheld.

On "the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter," the grammatical-historical note the present and future: "the things which are" (the present condition of the seven churches, addressed in the letters) and "the things which shall be hereafter" (the future events unveiled in the rest of the book) — many take this verse as the key to the book's structure, dividing it into the vision, the messages to the churches, and the prophetic visions of things to come. The verse displays the threefold command structuring the book.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the division — "write the things which thou hast seen, and the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter," the threefold outline: the vision, the present, the future (Barnes, Robertson); "the things which thou hast seen" the vision of the glorified Christ (Barnes, Ellicott); "the things which are" the present churches and "the things which shall be hereafter" the future, the key to the book's structure (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the structuring charge. The verse marks the seen: the vision. The verse displays the present: things which are. The verse displays the future: things hereafter. The verse displays the outline: threefold. The command displays the book divided into vision, present, and future. The verse displays the writing: commanded. The verse displays the present: the churches. The verse displays the future: unveiled. The threefold command structuring the book displays the key to its arrangement: write the things seen (the vision), the things which are (the churches), and the things which shall be hereafter (the future) — the threefold division of the Revelation. The verse displays the book's outline, opening with the mystery of the stars and lampstands (v.20).
20:
"The mystery of the seven stars which thou sawest in my right hand, and the seven golden candlesticks. The seven stars are the angels of the seven churches: and the seven candlesticks which thou sawest are the seven churches." The witnesses note the explanation of the stars and candlesticks — the crux of the angels of the churches. On "the seven candlesticks... are the seven churches," the grammatical voices note the plain explanation. Barnes and Robertson note the key given: "the seven candlesticks (lampstands)... are the seven churches" — Christ himself interprets the symbols: the lampstands are the seven churches, holding up the gospel light.

On "the seven stars are the angels of the seven churches," interpreters divide over the "angels." The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with Gill, hold that the "angels (angeloi — messengers)" of the churches are the human ministers/pastors of the churches — the presiding minister or chief teacher of each church (each letter addressed to "the angel" of a church being addressed to its minister, who represents the congregation); Barnes rejects the prelatical-bishop reading (no evidence of a bishop over a group of churches) and the "messengers sent to John" reading (Lord's view), taking "angel" as the pastor of each single church. A second view takes the "angels" as literal angels — the guardian angels of the churches. A third takes them as personifications/representatives of the churches themselves. The verse displays the explanation of the stars and candlesticks.

The witnesses agree the substance: Christ holds and explains the symbols — the lampstands are the churches, and the stars are their "angels," held in his right hand (Barnes, Robertson, Gill). But the "angels" divide them: the human ministers/pastors of the churches (the dominant Protestant view — Barnes, Robertson, Gill), or literal/guardian angels, or personified representatives of the churches. The verse displays the angels-of-the-churches crux. The verse marks the lampstands: the churches. The verse displays the stars: the angels. The verse displays the debate: human ministers or literal angels. The verse displays the holding: in Christ's hand. The explanation displays Christ interpreting the churches and their angels. The verse displays the candlesticks: churches. The verse displays the stars: angels. The verse displays the mystery: explained. The explanation of the stars and candlesticks displays the key to the vision and a crux: the seven lampstands are the seven churches, and the seven stars are their "angels" — read by the dominant Protestant view as the ministers/pastors of the churches (Barnes, Robertson, Gill), or as literal/guardian angels, or as representatives of the churches. The verse displays the angels of the churches, addressed in the seven letters (ch.2).
